topic: Not My Business by Niyi Osundare

subUnits.title: 8.1 Not My Business: Point of View, Metre, Comparisons, Exam Technique & Full Revision
notes: Not My Business by Niyi Osundare — Week 8
Key Concepts:
1. Point of View:
a) First Person Singular:
- The poem is written in the FIRST PERSON SINGULAR
- The speaker uses 'I,' 'my,' and 'me' throughout
- The entire poem is told from the perspective of this single
unnamed speaker who witnesses three acts of oppression
b) Effect 1 — Intimacy and Self-Incrimination:
- Because the poem is in the first person, we are inside the speaker's head
- We hear his reasoning directly, in his own words
- This makes his selfishness feel very close — we cannot dismiss it
as someone else's failure
- We are forced to sit inside the perspective of a person making
the wrong choice repeatedly and hear him justify it
- This is uncomfortable — which is exactly what Osundare intends
c) Effect 2 — The Speaker as Everyman:
- The speaker has no name, no face, no specific identity
- He is 'I' — which in poetry can mean anyone
- By using the first person with an unnamed speaker, Osundare
suggests this speaker is not a unique villain but an ordinary person
- Any ordinary person could fall into the same trap of
self-interested silence
- The 'I' of the poem could be you
d) Effect 3 — The Reader's Growing Judgment:
- As the poem progresses, the reader's judgment of the 'I' speaker
grows harsher with each stanza
- By the final stanza, when the jeep arrives, the reader feels
no sympathy — only the grim satisfaction of a lesson learned too late
2. Metre and Versification:
a) Free Verse:
- 'Not My Business' is written in FREE VERSE
- This means it has:
* No fixed metre — no regular pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables
* No fixed rhyme scheme — no deliberate pattern of end rhymes
* No fixed line length — lines vary in the number of syllables
b) Count the Syllables to See the Irregularity:
- 'They picked Akanni up one morning' = 9 syllables
- 'Beat him soft like clay' = 5 syllables
- 'And stuffed him down the belly' = 8 syllables
- 'Of a waiting jeep.' = 5 syllables
- 'What business of mine is it' = 7 syllables
- 'So long as they don't take the yam' = 9 syllables
- 'From my savouring mouth?' = 6 syllables
c) What Creates Rhythm Without Metre?:
- Even though the poem has no fixed metre, it has a very strong
rhythmic quality. This rhythm is created by:
1. The Refrain: The three-line refrain repeats after every stanza
like a drumbeat. This is the poem's primary rhythmic device.
2. Parallel Structure: Each of the first three stanzas follows
the same shape — narrative (4 lines) + refrain (3 lines).
This parallelism creates a powerful structural rhythm.
3. Short, Punchy Lines: Many lines are very short and direct —
'Beat him soft like clay,' 'A waiting jeep.' These lines have
the punching rhythm of a protest chant.
d) Why Did Osundare Choose Free Verse?:
- Accessibility: Osundare's mission is to write poetry that every
Nigerian can understand. A poem in strict iambic pentameter would
feel formal and distant. Free verse feels like ordinary speech —
like a man telling you a story on the street.
- Urgency: Free verse is the natural rhythm of urgent, direct speech.
The poem reads almost like a political speech — and that urgency
is essential to its impact.
- Content Demands Freedom: A poem about the suppression of freedom
and the silencing of ordinary people should not imprison itself
in rigid formal metre. Free verse enacts the poem's values:
directness, honesty, and freedom from artificial constraints.
3. Exam Technique — The PEE Method:
Point — Evidence — Explanation
For any question about a literary device, theme, or technique,
use the PEE method:
P — Point: Make your point clearly. State what you are going to argue.
E — Evidence: Provide a direct quotation from the poem.
E — Explanation: Explain how/why the quotation proves your point,
and what effect it has on the reader.
WORKED EXAMPLE — Question: 'How does Osundare use symbolism
in Not My Business?'
POINT: Osundare uses the symbol of the yam to represent the speaker's
selfish self-interest.
EVIDENCE: The speaker repeatedly asks, 'So long as they don't take the yam
/ From my savouring mouth?'
EXPLANATION: In Nigerian culture, yam is a staple food associated with
prosperity and sustenance. Here, the yam represents the speaker's personal
comfort, material security, and narrow self-interest. The word 'savouring'
— meaning enjoying with great pleasure — reveals that the speaker is not
merely surviving; he is actively indulging himself while his neighbours
suffer. By making the yam the speaker's only concern, Osundare reduces
his entire moral worldview to a single mouthful of food, exposing the
absurdity and danger of his selfishness.
4. Complete Revision Summary:
POET & CONTEXT:
- Niyi Osundare — born 1947, Ikere-Ekiti, Nigeria
- Poem from: Songs of the Marketplace (1983)
- Context: Written during era of Nigerian military dictatorship
CHARACTERS:
- Akanni (Yoruba) — arrested and beaten
- Danladi (Hausa) — taken in a night raid; lengthy absence
- Chinwe (Igbo) — sacked from 20-year job
- The Speaker — unnamed, ordinary Nigerian; self-interested; eventually victimized
- 'They' — faceless agents of state oppression
THEMES:
- Civic apathy and political indifference — DOMINANT THEME
- Political oppression and state brutality
- The consequence of silence
- Self-interest versus the common good
- Community solidarity and its absence
TONE AND MOOD:
- Tone: Satirical, ironic, didactic (teaching)
- Mood: Growing unease (stanzas 1-3); cold terror and inevitability (stanza 4)
LITERARY DEVICES:
- Refrain — 'What business of mine is it...' repeated after each stanza
- Situational irony — the speaker's silence leads to his own victimization
- Dramatic irony — the reader sees the speaker's doom coming before he does
- Simile — 'Beat him soft like clay' (dehumanization of Akanni)
- Symbolism — Yam (self-interest); Jeep (state oppression); Knock (consequences)
- Personification — 'belly of a waiting jeep'; 'bewildered road'
- Repetition/Anaphora — 'They picked... They came...';
'A knock on the door froze my hungry hand' (x2)
- Alliteration — 's' sounds (sinister undercurrent);
'b' sounds (brutality)
- Anticlimax/Bathos — the refrain's grand philosophy collapses in stanza 4
- Enjambment — final stanza creates breathlessness and inevitability
STRUCTURE AND FORM:
- 4 stanzas. Stanzas 1-3: narrative (4 lines) + refrain (3 lines).
Stanza 4: 6 lines, NO refrain
- Free verse — no fixed metre or rhyme scheme
- Rhythmic devices: refrain, parallel structure, short punchy lines
- The missing refrain in stanza 4 is the poem's most important structural device
POINT OF VIEW:
- First person singular ('I') — unnamed speaker
- Effect: Intimacy; the speaker is an Everyman;
reader's judgment builds with each stanza
Nigerian Context:
- The poem reflects real experiences under Nigerian military rule
- Night raids, arbitrary arrests, forced disappearances were common
- The poem warns against the culture of silence that enabled oppression
- WAEC and JAMB frequently test understanding of this context
Conclusion:
- 'Not My Business' is a powerful critique of civic apathy during military rule
- The poem uses free verse, refrain, irony, and symbolism to teach a moral lesson
- The structural break in stanza 4 (absence of refrain) is the poem's climax
- The poem teaches: if you don't stand up for others, no one will stand up for you
- Mastery supports understanding of political poetry, social justice, and civic responsibility
- This poem is essential for WAEC Paper 3 and JAMB Literature examinations

quiz.q: The poem 'Not My Business' is written in which point of view?
quiz.explanation: The poem is written in first person singular ('I,' 'my,' 'me') throughout, told from the perspective of an unnamed speaker.
'Not My Business' is written in free verse — no fixed metre, no regular rhyme scheme, no fixed line length.

quiz.q: The primary rhythmic device in the poem is:
quiz.explanation: The refrain ('What business of mine is it...') repeats after each stanza like a drumbeat, creating the poem's primary rhythm.

quiz.q: Why did Osundare choose free verse for this poem?
opts: To make it more difficult to understand
To create accessibility and urgency, like ordinary speech
To follow traditional English poetic forms
To make it easier to memorize
quiz.explanation: Osundare chose free verse to make the poem accessible to all Nigerians and to create the urgency of direct, political speech.

quiz.q: The absence of the refrain in stanza 4 is significant because:
opts: It shows the speaker can no longer ask the question — it IS his business now
It makes the poem more musical
It was censored by the government
quiz.explanation: The absence of the refrain is the poem's most powerful structural device — the speaker can no longer ask 'What business of mine is it?' because the jeep has come for him.

quiz.q: In comparing 'Not My Business' with Maya Angelou's 'Still I Rise,' the key contrast is:
opts: Angelou's speaker triumphs; Osundare's speaker is defeated
quiz.explanation: The key contrast is that Angelou's speaker actively resists and triumphs, while Osundare's speaker fails to resist and is defeated.

quiz.q: The PEE method for answering exam questions stands for:
quiz.explanation: PEE stands for Point (make your argument), Evidence (quote from the text), Explanation (explain how the evidence proves your point).

quiz.q: The speaker in the poem is unnamed to make him:
opts: An Everyman figure — he could be anyone
quiz.explanation: By keeping the speaker unnamed, Osundare makes him universal — he could be any ordinary Nigerian, even you.

opts: Four stanzas; stanzas 1-3 have narrative + refrain, stanza 4 has no refrain
quiz.explanation: The poem has four stanzas. Stanzas 1-3 follow the pattern: narrative (4 lines) + refrain (3 lines). Stanza 4 has 6 lines and NO refrain.

quiz.q: For WAEC and JAMB exams, students should know about 'Not My Business':
opts: The poet, collection, context, themes, devices, and be able to write essay answers
quiz.explanation: For exams, students must know: poet (Niyi Osundare), collection (Songs of the Marketplace, 1983), context (military dictatorship), themes, literary devices, and be able to write structured essay answers using the PEE method.
